does: This paper introduces seven detection techniques for identifying prompt-injection attacks in large language models, moving beyond standard regex pattern matching and fine-tuned transformer approaches. The seven methods are: forensic linguistics, materials-science fatigue analysis, deception technology, local-sequence alignment, mechanism design, spectral signal analysis, and taint tracking. Three techniques are implemented in prompt-shield v0.4.1 and evaluated through ablation studies across six datasets. The work demonstrates that these cross-domain techniques address specific failure modes of existing detectors, particularly their susceptibility to paraphrased attacks and adaptive adversaries, which have been shown in recent work to bypass published defenses at greater than 50% success rates.
connects: This work builds on emerging recognition that current prompt-injection defenses have fundamental limitations. Recent NAACL Findings research exposed how fine-tuned transformer classifiers fail under adaptive attacks, while regex-based methods miss paraphrased malicious inputs. The paper engages with the broader adversarial machine learning literature examining robustness against adaptive adversaries and draws on established security paradigms from network defense and compiler design. It responds to the growing evidence that domain-specific solutions—like those developed in bioinformatics, forensic analysis, and network security—may offer complementary insights unavailable to LLM-native approaches.
enables: By introducing detection methods from seven disparate domains, this work opens pathways for future cross-disciplinary security research in LLM systems. The open-source implementation in prompt-shield creates infrastructure for community-driven improvements and empirical testing of additional techniques. The approach suggests that prompt-injection defense might benefit from borrowing established robustness mechanisms from mature security and technical fields, potentially leading to detector ensembles that are harder to bypass uniformly. This framework could inspire similar cross-domain transfers in other AI safety problems and establishes practical benchmarks for evaluating detector resilience against adaptive adversaries.
